b: A hotel front desk: guests never enter the kitchen (backends). The desk checks keys (auth), enforces house rules (rate limits), answers from memory (cache), and forwards requests to the right department (routing).
Speed limit (right now) vs monthly fuel allowance (long term). You can obey one and still violate the other.
New book edition (readers choose it; story changes) vs a reprint fixing typos (silently replaces stock; nothing breaks).
rate-limit-by-key with counter-key=@(context.Request.IpAddress) or a JWT claim — a constant key makes every caller share one bucket.
A deli ticket machine: any free clerk serves the next ticket; nobody is served twice; a rush just makes the line longer (load leveling).
A magazine subscription list: print once, every subscriber gets a copy, some only take the sports edition (filters).
The post office's undeliverable-mail shelf: after failed attempts, the letter waits with a note saying why, for a human to investigate.
A coat check: pass the small ticket (blob reference) around the party, not the winter coat (huge payload).
Doorbell (fact happened, react if you care) vs courier package (payload the sender expects processed, signature required).

f: Config: lock duration vs processing time
b: If processing exceeds the lock, the message redelivers mid-work → duplicates. Renew locks or extend duration; always code idempotent handlers.
TTL expiry with the flag off silently DELETES messages; with it on, they land in the DLQ where monitoring can catch them.
Office keycards: who you are (principal) + which doors open (role) + which floors (scope). Cards go to teams (groups), not individuals.
The master-key sign-out sheet: borrow with a reason, manager approves, auto-return in 4 hours, every borrow logged.
A badge issued by the building itself — the app never carries a password, so there's nothing to steal or leak to GitHub.
'Reader' on a storage account ≠ reading blobs. Data access needs DataActions roles like Storage Blob Data Reader, scoped as low as the container.
curl 169.254.169.254/metadata/identity/oauth2/token?resource=https://storage.azure.com/ — the resource/audience must match the service you call.
A private elevator installed straight into your office: the public lobby is bricked over, and the building directory (private DNS) routes visitors to your floor.
An airport hub: every regional flight connects through it, where security (firewall), customs (gateways), and the control tower (DNS/monitoring) live once.
A private rail line vs an armored truck on the public highway — both safe, but the rail line never touches public roads and carries far more freight.
Front Door = global concierge. App Gateway = building receptionist with metal detector (WAF). Load Balancer = elevator dispatcher. Traffic Manager = phone directory (DNS).
(1) privatelink zone exists, (2) zone linked to the consuming VNet, (3) DNS zone group creates the A record. Custom DNS must forward to 168.63.129.16.
Peering is non-transitive — add UDRs (0.0.0.0/0 or spoke CIDRs → hub firewall) to route between spokes through the hub.

f: 🧒 Scale to zero in plain words
b: Motion-sensor lights: dark (free) until someone walks in, with a half-second flicker (cold start) as they switch on.
Test the new recipe on 10% of tables; complaints → old recipe returns instantly; praise → roll it out to every table.
Open supermarket checkouts because six people are queuing (queue depth), not because the clock says 5 pm.
revisions-mode multiple + ingress traffic set --revision-weight A=90 B=10; single-revision mode ignores weights.
scale-rule-type azure-servicebus with queueName/namespace/messageCount metadata + connection auth secret; min-replicas 0 enables scale-to-zero.
Airport ID check (who you are) vs boarding pass (what you may do: this flight, this seat). Both checks, in that order.
A valet key: starts the car but not the trunk (limited scopes), expires soon, and you never hand over your real key (password).
A nightclub where every door has a bouncer: front-door entry opens nothing else; wristbands match what you paid for; cameras assume someone snuck in.
Hotel safe with a logbook: valuables out from under mattresses (config files), every opening logged, can't discard the safe while full (purge protection).
client_credentials + scope=api://your-api/.default (rolls up app roles). Named scopes in that flow fail — they're for delegated auth.
Two cloud-only accounts excluded from Conditional Access, long random passwords, alert on every sign-in — insurance against CA/MFA lockout.
Photo backups: one shoebox (LRS), three rooms (ZRS), a copy mailed to grandma — slightly delayed (GRS), rooms AND grandma (GZRS).
Closet (Hot) → attic (Cool) → storage unit (Cold) → bank vault: cheap but retrieval needs an appointment (Archive rehydration).
Group chat: Strong = nobody sees a message until everyone can. Session = you always see your own instantly (default). Eventual = all arrive, maybe out of order.
Filing cabinets: file orders by customer and lookups fly; file everything under '2026' and one drawer jams (hot partition) while others sit empty.
'How long until the shop reopens?' (RTO) vs 'how many receipts did we lose?' (RPO). Price both fears separately.
Mirror vs photo album: the mirror instantly shows the ketchup stain too (corruption); the album lets you return to before the spill.
Christmas lights: serial = every extra bulb can kill the string (multiply, availability drops). Parallel = two strings side by side (availability jumps).
Cold (rebuild from IaC, hours, cheapest) → warm (scaled-down, minutes) → hot/active-active (seconds, priciest). RTO picks the rung.
az sql db restore creates a NEW database at the chosen time — plan the cutover/rename step and rehearse it; that time is your real RTO.
Soft delete, versioning, immutability only cover events AFTER enablement, and retention must exceed detection time (found on day 9 > 7-day retention = gone).
Car dashboard (instant, cheap, alarms) vs trip journal (rich history for later questions — KQL).
A barcode that follows one package end-to-end: scan one order ID and see every warehouse, truck, and doorstep it touched.
Door keys (who may build) vs building codes (what anyone may build). Codes stop wooden shacks even for landowners.
The cluster reads its recipe book (Git) and cooks accordingly; fix the book, not the kitchen — rollback = revert the page, and the book's margin notes are your audit log.
PHS (resilient, zero infra) + Conditional Access baseline + PIM JIT + break-glass + policy-carrying management groups. License P2 only for admins.
EU data on EU SQL servers, failover groups paired within the geography — DR that respects GDPR by architecture, not paperwork.
Zones for common failures; warm standby region for disasters; ASR for legacy VMs; failover groups for SQL; immutable GRS backups for ransomware; quarterly drills.
Spoke-per-customer Bicep stamps, all egress via hub Firewall Premium, private endpoints + policy deny on public access, central logs with resource-context RBAC.
OIDC federation (CI) + workload identity (pods) + private ACR with scanning + approved-registry policy + Flux GitOps = zero stored secrets, full provenance.

f: Lab habit that wins the exam
b: After every hands-on step ask: which requirement (cost, RTO, isolation, least privilege) did this flag serve? AZ-305 tests the why, not the syntax.
'Minimize cost' → cheapest compliant. 'Minimize admin effort' → most managed/PaaS. 'Most secure' → private + MI + JIT. 'Minimize changes' → lift-and-shift options.
